headline: The Rankings Game Malaysian Universities Cannot Win
context: Five public universities spent RM340M on internationalization initiatives to climb QS rankings while graduate employability within Malaysia fell to 79%.

cards.big: Rankings went up. Graduate employment went down.
cards.sub: QS rewards research citations and international faculty ratios, not whether graduates get jobs.

cards.big: QS methodology: 30% weight on reputation surveys, 0% on employability.
cards.sub: The metric universities optimize for does not measure what students need.

cards.big: RM340M on internationalization. RM12M on industry placement programmes.
cards.sub: The funding ratio reveals the actual priority: prestige over outcomes.

cards.big: AUKU still restricts student political activity on campus.
cards.sub: Rankings reward academic freedom. Malaysian law restricts it. The contradiction is structural.

cards.big: Are we improving education or improving our position on someone else's scoreboard?
Rankings measure what rankers value. That is not the same as what Malaysia needs from its universities.
